model_narrative: We cut finished paper products of several widths out of raw paper rolls that all share a single standard width. Each finished width has a required quantity that must be met. A raw roll can be sliced according to any of a number of predefined cutting patterns, and for each pattern we decide how many raw rolls to cut that way. We also keep track of the total number of raw rolls consumed. The objective is to minimize the total number of raw rolls used.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the set of finished products to be cut, each identified by its required width

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the set of available cutting patterns; each pattern is one way of slicing a single raw roll into finished widths, and some entries are unused empty slots that produce nothing

params.name: r
params.doc: the width of a raw paper roll, in width units; a single scalar equal to 100

params.name: w
params.doc: the width of each finished product, in the same width units as the raw roll

params.name: d
params.doc: the number of finished pieces of each width that must be produced

params.name: aip
params.doc: the number of finished pieces of a given width that one application of a given cutting pattern yields; zero for combinations a pattern does not produce, including the unused empty patterns

vars.name: xp
vars.doc: the number of times each cutting pattern is applied, i.e. how many raw rolls are cut according to that pattern; a whole number that cannot be negative

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total number of raw rolls used across all patterns

constraints.name: xp[p]
constraints.intent: The total number of raw rolls used is the number of rolls cut across every pattern added together. Set the total rolls used equal to the sum over all patterns of how many times each pattern is applied.

constraints.name: aip[i,
constraints.intent: Every finished width must be produced in at least the quantity required. For each finished width, the total number of pieces of that width yielded across all patterns, counting how many times each pattern is applied and how many pieces of that width each application produces, must be at least the required quantity for that width.

query: We're looking at spreading wear across the cutting line so the two generated mixed layouts don't get hammered together. There's a proposal on the table to keep the combined run on those two mixed patterns under 280 rolls between them and let the dedicated single-width cuts pick up the rest. Where does the total roll count come out?

query: We'd like the 36-wide product to lean on its own dedicated double-cut rather than riding along on the mixed layout p5 that also throws off 36-wide. There's an idea to require that the dedicated 36-wide pattern p2 always run at least as many rolls as the mixed pattern p5. What would that do to the total roll count?

query: We're considering a blanket setup-fairness rule so no single cutting pattern dominates the schedule. The proposal is to cap every pattern at 170 rolls across the board. With that uniform ceiling on each pattern, where does the total roll count land?

query: It bugs me that we've got these perfectly good dedicated single-width patterns for the 36-wide, 31-wide and 14-wide products, and the plan barely runs them — it just leans on the two mixed cuts. Shouldn't the schedule put real volume through those three dedicated patterns, say at least 200 rolls between them? What's the model seeing that keeps it from making that move?

query: The whole mixed-cut load is lopsided onto pattern p6 — it's running way more than the other generated layout p5. Couldn't we balance the two and hold p6 to at most a third of the combined run across both mixed patterns? What's the tradeoff keeping the optimizer from spreading them more evenly?

query: A couple of patterns are running enormous batches while the rest sit nearly idle — that feels like all our eggs in two baskets. Wouldn't a firm per-pattern ceiling of 140 rolls on every layout spread the work out without much cost? What's the model giving up to keep those two patterns running so hot?

query: I want to even out the two mixed layouts and rein in the setup churn on p5 at the same time. Couldn't we both hold pattern p6 to no more than 45 percent of the combined run across the two mixed patterns and keep p5 itself under 180 rolls? Each of those caps looks harmless on its own, so what's the model giving up when we ask for both together?
